text: The surgeon begins creating a flap laterally, aiming to access the correct anatomical plane for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon begins creating a flap laterally, aiming to enter the correct anatomical plane for the procedure.
The surgeon holds a forced bipolar instrument, which was accidentally opened, though it is not typically used for routine inguinal hernia repairs.
The surgeon attempts to access a plane near the retro-rectus space, manipulating tissue layers to find the correct surgical plane for hernia repair.
The surgeon navigates through the pre-peritoneal space, revealing a distinct tissue layer that is not part of the retro-rectal space.
The surgeon identifies multiple tissue layers in the pre-peritoneal space, including the peritoneum and additional layers, while noting the complexity of the anatomical structure.
The surgeon identifies multiple layers in the surgical field, including the peritoneum and additional tissue layers, while discussing the complexity of the anatomical planes.
The surgeon creates a broad flap, revealing multiple layers of tissue that become apparent during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon works broadly to open a flap, revealing multiple layers of tissue that become visible during the procedure.

The surgeon attempts to sweep small blood vessels off the peritoneum, noting the thinness of the peritoneal layer. Care is taken not to force the issue when the peritoneum is very thin, aiming to work directly on the peritoneal surface.
The surgeon attempts to sweep small blood vessels off the peritoneum, noting the thinness of the peritoneal layer. Care is taken not to force the issue if the vessels do not easily detach.
The surgeon navigates between the testicular vessels and the sac, aiming to stay outside the retro-rectus space to minimize vascular encounters while working in the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon carefully navigates outside of the retro-rectus space, aiming to stay within a less vascular plane during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon navigates into the retro-rectus space as the posterior rectus sheath becomes less distinct at lower levels, revealing the presence of gaseous structures in this area.
Below the arcuate line, the surgeon identifies two distinct layers between the peritoneum and the rectus muscle, facilitating plane development during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon identifies two distinct tissue layers between the peritoneum and the rectus muscle below the arcuate line, facilitating plane development during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies Cooper's ligament, visible in the surgical field, and notes the absence of excessive fat covering it. The dissection proceeds in a superficial plane, achieving clear areolar exposure around the ligament.

The surgeon identifies Cooper's ligament, visible in the surgical field. There is minimal fat covering the ligament, allowing the surgeon to maintain a superficial dissection plane without needing to go more ventral. The areolar tissue is exposed clearly, facilitating the dissection.
The surgeon navigates towards the obturator space, carefully avoiding damage to surrounding structures, while managing occasional bleeding from torn blood vessels.

The surgeon navigates to the obturator space, carefully avoiding damage to the surrounding structures and preventing bleeding from the coronamortis. Large blood vessels are visible within the obturator space.

The surgeon carefully exposes the obturator space, revealing large vessels that must be avoided to prevent bleeding from the coronamortis. The surgeon pulls the tissue to expose the areolar tissue for precise dissection.

The surgeon carefully pulls and lifts the tissue to expose the areolar tissue for dissection, ensuring the plane is visible even in tighter areas. This technique is applicable in most redo situations.
The surgeon lifts the tissue to identify the surgical plane, revealing the distinct areolar tissue that is visible even in redo hernia repair situations.
The surgeon identifies and works within the areolar tissue plane to achieve optimal exposure, carefully navigating to avoid critical structures.
The surgeon identifies and works within the areolar tissue plane, using this approach to safely expose anatomical structures while avoiding critical areas.

The transverse salus sling is visible wrapping around the area, requiring removal. The surgeon uses cautery near the core structures, with the testicular vessels safely tucked away at the back, while the front area remains clear for dissection.

The surgeon carefully removes small vessels and fatty tissue from the hernia sac, ensuring that all visible structures in the front are detached from the sac. The presence of fat indicates being in the incorrect plane, prompting further dissection to clear the area.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes small vessels from the surface of the sac. Visible fat indicates the wrong dissection plane, prompting adjustment to ensure proper tissue separation.
The surgeon uses cautery to detach tissue from the hernia sac edge, carefully following the contour of the sac to ensure complete removal of surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses small amounts of cautery to detach tissue from the edge of the sack, ensuring all surrounding structures are removed from the sack's surface.
The surgeon examines the surgical field, identifying what appears to be the hernia sac with a distinct edge. The surgeon proceeds to dissect around the sac, removing small vessels from the area.
The surgeon examines the surgical field, identifying and dissecting around the sac-like structure. Small vessels are visible and are being carefully removed from the area.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to strip small vessels from the hernia sac edge, avoiding the use of two graspers typically employed in laparoscopic techniques. The small vessels are removed, while care is taken to preserve the larger vessel.

The surgeon uses cautery to dissect along the edge of the sack, removing small vessels without the need for laparoscopic graspers. The vessels are stripped away, while care is taken to preserve larger structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects within the natural areolar plane, pulling tissue towards the nine o'clock position and scraping down to enhance exposure and ensure safe dissection. No significant structures like the 'baz' are visible, indicating the area is safe for tissue removal.

The surgeon follows the natural areolar plane, pulling tissue towards nine o'clock and scraping down to enhance exposure and dissection. The edge of the tissue is clearly visible, and the area is deemed safe for further dissection as the 'baz' is not present.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the hernia sac and uses a technique of pulling to nine o'clock and scraping down to three o'clock to expose the juncture. The surgeon lifts the tissue and divides it, demonstrating precise anatomical dissection.

The surgeon pulls tissue towards nine o'clock and scrapes down towards three o'clock, revealing a juncture that can be divided. The surgeon lifts the tissue and proceeds to divide it, demonstrating anatomical knowledge during dissection.

The surgeon carefully lifts tissue away and uses cautery, ensuring that the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve and femoral branch, located deeper in the surgical field, remain unharmed. The technique minimizes the risk of nerve injury while controlling bleeding.

The surgeon lifts tissue away and uses cautery, ensuring that the lateral femoral cutaneous and femoral branch nerves, located deeper in the surgical field, remain unharmed. The technique minimizes the risk of nerve injury while controlling bleeding.

The surgeon identifies the peritoneal edge and peels back tissue, maintaining a bloodless field. The testicular vessels are visible, allowing for cautious use of cautery to ensure hemostasis. The surgeon plans to tuck the mesh two centimeters down into the obturator space, ensuring it is sandwiched between the bladder and surrounding structures. The mesh is positioned to prevent any tissue from sneaking behind it. The surgeon inspects the illopubic tract, confirming the absence of significant fat anterior to it, indicating completion of this step. A cord structure is noted, and the surgeon discusses the rare occurrence of retroperitoneal fat herniating around the testicular vessels, which can lead to recurrence if not addressed.
The surgeon observes a clear view of the surgical field without bleeding, identifying the peritoneal edge and peeling it back, with the option to extend dissection towards the pancreas.

The surgeon lifts the tissue, maintaining a bloodless field, and uses cautery near the testicular vessels to ensure hemostasis. The mesh is positioned two centimeters down, aiming to tuck it into the obturator space, allowing it to be sandwiched between the bladder and surrounding structures.

The surgeon ensures a smooth transition for the mesh placement, tucking it securely into the corner to prevent any structures from sneaking behind it. The mesh is positioned with minimal dissection to facilitate its placement. The surgeon identifies the illopubic tract, confirming the absence of excessive fat anterior to it, indicating completion of this step.
The surgeon identifies a cord structure and examines the fat surrounding the testicular vessels, noting the presence of retroperitoneal fat herniating down, which can lead to recurrence if missed.
The surgeon uses cautery to carefully separate the vas deferens from surrounding tissues, ensuring a clear transition to prevent herniation. The iliac vein is visible but not exposed further.

The surgeon uses cautery to carefully free the vas deferens, visible in the surgical field, while ensuring a smooth transition to prevent herniation. The iliac vein is noted but not exposed during this step.

The surgeon lifts and accentuates the corner to identify the precise area for division, crossing the midline to ensure the mesh will lay smoothly. The mega suture cut needle driver is prepared for use.

The surgeon lifts tissue away to accentuate a corner, identifying the precise location for division. The midline is crossed, and additional adjustments are made to ensure the mesh lays smoothly. A mega suture cut needle driver is prepared for use.

The surgeon uses a force bipolar instrument, initially in fenestrated bipolar mode, to grasp bowel tissue. Upon activating the yellow pedal, the instrument switches to pro grasp mode, suitable for handling fascia or old mesh.

The surgeon uses a dual grip force bipolar instrument, initially in fenestrated bipolar mode to grasp bowel. Upon stepping on the yellow pedal, the instrument switches to pro grasp mode, suitable for handling fascia or tough old mesh.

The surgeon adjusts the mesh, ensuring it lays flat and tucks in the corner to prevent lifting. The peritoneal edge is visible and is pulled back slightly to ensure proper placement. The surgical field is cleared as the scope is cleaned.

The surgeon adjusts the mesh, ensuring it lays flat and tucks into the corner to prevent lifting. The peritoneal edge is visible, and the surgeon pulls it back slightly to ensure proper placement of the mesh.
The surgeon places one fixation point in Cooper's ligament and another in the left upper quadrant to secure the mesh, ensuring it remains flat and does not fold or shift.

The surgeon adjusts the mesh to prevent it from rotating counterclockwise, pushing it down and suturing it in place. Two stitches are applied to secure the mesh, while the camera is positioned far from the peritoneal flap, above the umbilicus.

The surgeon adjusts the mesh to prevent it from rotating counterclockwise, pushing it down and suturing it in place. Two stitches are placed to secure the mesh, while the camera is positioned far from the peritoneal flap, above the umbilicus.
The surgeon increases the pneumoperitoneum pressure back up to fifteen, allowing for better visualization. The mesh is observed to be tucked nicely in place, indicating successful positioning.
The surgeon increases the pneumoperitoneum pressure back up to fifteen, revealing the mesh tucked neatly in place within the surgical field.

The surgeon adjusts the position of the 3D Max mesh, ensuring the edge is not adhered to surrounding tissue. The mesh is cut flush to prevent tension that could lead to a tail causing bowel obstruction. The needle driver and sutures are removed from the surgical field.
